title: 七面钟之谜
source_file: data\raw\detectiveqa\novel_data_en\144-七面钟之谜-阿加莎克里斯蒂.txt
summary:
Jimmy Sycamore and Eileen Bündel discuss the events of the previous night. Jimmy proposes a theory that the Countess and Bourne acted as accomplices in a theft: he suggests the Countess provided O'Roke with a sleeping pill, allowing Bourne to enter O'Roke's room via the ivy to steal documents and throw them down to the Countess. Jimmy notes the plan failed because he was waiting at the bottom, forcing the Countess to hide behind a library screen to avoid him. Bündel disputes this, arguing the explanation is too convenient and that Bourne was merely a scapegoat intended to divert attention from a mysterious figure known as "seven o'clock," whom she believes is a guest at the party.

Jimmy questions whether Inspector Butler had a plan to use the Countess to catch someone else. They also discuss the departures of Sir Stanley Digby and O'Roke, with Jimmy suggesting O'Roke is also a problematic figure. Bündel notes that the Bartlett Inspector was definitely not present at seven o'clock.

George Lomax approaches Bündel and Eileen, delivering a long speech regarding traditional values, family, and dignity. He expresses a desire to act as a political mentor to Bündel by providing her with political economics books. After George leaves, Bill arrives and questions why George was touching Bündel's hand. Bündel expresses concern that George's interest will trap her in political obligations, but Bill dismisses her fears, noting that George does not approve of women in parliament.
